Given ϕ<<KE

hcλincident≈KE

hcλincident=p22m ......(i)

λ'=49λ

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement-I: The orbitals having same energy are called as degenerate orbitals.
Statement-II: In hydrogen atom, 3p and 3 d orbitals are not degenerate orbitals.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement-I: The orbitals having same energy are called as degenerate orbitals.
Statement-II: In hydrogen atom, 3p and 3 d orbitals are not degenerate orbitals.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given
options: Statement-I is true but Statement-II is false
Both Statement-I and Statement-II are true.
Both Statement-I and Statement-II are false
Statement-I is false but Statement-II is true
solution:
Degenerate orbitals are the orbitals of the same subshell having equal energies. When the electrons of the orbitals are not influenced by any external factors like an electric field or magnetic field, they have same energies.

For a single electron species like hydrogen atom, the energy depends upon the principal quantum number 'n' only. Hence, 3s, 3p and 3d oribitals have same energy. So, statement II is false.

Statement I is correct definition of degenerate orbitals.

Hence, the answer is option A.

q: The isoelectronic set of ions is:
question: The isoelectronic set of ions is:
solution:
In Isoelectronic species, number of electrons should be same
Number of electrons present in Li+= 3-1 = 2
Number of electrons present in Na+= 11-1 = 10
(A) F-=9+1=10e-
Li+=3-1=2e-
Na+=11-1=10e-
Mg+2=12-2=10e-
(B) N-3=7+3=10e-
Li+=3-1=2e-
Mg+2=12-2=10e-
Na+=11-1=10e-
(C) F-= 9+1 = 10
O2-= 8+2 = 10
N3-= 7+3 = 10
Mg2+=12-2=10
(d) Li+=3-1=2e-
Na+=11-1=10e-
O-2=8+2=10e-
F-=9+1=10e-
Hence, N3-, O2-, F-, Na+ are isoelectronic.

question: The four quantum numbers for the electron in the outer most orbital of potassium (atomic no. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>19</mn></math>) are
solution:
The set of numbers used to describe the position and energy of the electron in an atom are called quantum numbers. There are four quantum numbers, namely, principal, azimuthal, magnetic and spin quantum numbers.

Electronic configuration of K is:

K= Atomic number=191s22s22p63s23p64s1

The outermost electron is in 4s1

∴ n=4, l=0, m=0, s=+12

Hence, the answer is option B.

question:
The total number of isoelectronic species from the given set is ________.

O2-, F–, Al, Mg2+, Na+, O+, Mg, Al3+, F
solution:
Isoelectronic are those which have the same number of electrons. Let us write the number of electrons from the given species.

Species No. of electrons
O–2  10
F- 10
Al 13
Mg2+ 10
Na+ 10
O+ 7
Mg 12
Al3+ 10
F 9

Hence five species among the given are isoelectronic species.

∴ mΔV.Δx=h4π

m=6.6269.6×π×10-1

m=0.02198kg

m=21.98gm

nearest integer =22

subject: Chemistry
q:
What is the energy (in J atom$^{-1}$) required for the following process?
$Li^{2+}(g) \rightarrow Li^{3+}(g) + e^-$
(Take the ionization energy for the H atom in the ground state as $2.18 \times 10^{-18}$ J atom$^{-1}$)
question:
What is the energy (in J atom$^{-1}$) required for the following process?
$Li^{2+}(g) \rightarrow Li^{3+}(g) + e^-$
(Take the ionization energy for the H atom in the ground state as $2.18 \times 10^{-18}$ J atom$^{-1}$)
solution:
The given process $Li^{2+}(g) \rightarrow Li^{3+}(g) + e^-$ represents the ionization of the hydrogen-like ion $Li^{2+}$ from its ground state.

The ionization energy for a hydrogen-like species is given by the formula:
$IE = IE_{H} \times Z^2$

For $Li^{2+}$, the atomic number $Z = 3$.

Given the ionization energy of the hydrogen atom $IE_{H} = 2.18 \times 10^{-18}$ J atom$^{-1}$.

Substituting the values:
$IE = 2.18 \times 10^{-18} \times (3)^2$

$IE = 2.18 \times 10^{-18} \times 9$

$IE = 19.62 \times 10^{-18}$ J atom$^{-1}$

$IE = 1.962 \times 10^{-17}$ J atom$^{-1}$

Answer: $1.962 \times 10^{-17}$

$$

subject: Chemistry
q: The energy of first (lowest) Balmer line of H atom is $x \mathrm{~J}$. The energy (in J) of second Balmer line of H atom is :
question: The energy of first (lowest) Balmer line of H atom is $x \mathrm{~J}$. The energy (in J) of second Balmer line of H atom is :
solution:
First Balmer line:
transition n=3 to n=2, energy $E_1 = 13.6(\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{9}) = 13.6 \times \frac{5}{36} = x$ J.
Second Balmer line:
transition n=4 to n=2, energy $E_2 = 13.6(\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{16}) = 13.6 \times \frac{3}{16}$ J.
Ratio: $\frac{E_2}{E_1} = \frac{\frac{3}{16}}{\frac{5}{36}} = \frac{3}{16} \times \frac{36}{5} = \frac{27}{20} = 1.35$.
Thus second Balmer line energy is $1.35x$ J.

subject: Chemistry
q: If shortest wavelength of hydrogen atom in Lyman series is $x$, then longest wavelength in Balmer series of $\text{He}^+$ is:
question: If shortest wavelength of hydrogen atom in Lyman series is $x$, then longest wavelength in Balmer series of $\text{He}^+$ is:
solution:
For the shortest wavelength in the Lyman series of the hydrogen atom ($Z=1$), the transition is from $n_2 = \infty$ to $n_1 = 1$.

$\dfrac{1}{x} = R(1)^2 \left(\dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{\infty^2}\right) = R$

This gives $R = \dfrac{1}{x}$.

For the longest wavelength in the Balmer series of $\text{He}^+$ ($Z=2$), the transition is from $n_2 = 3$ to $n_1 = 2$.

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R(2)^2 \left(\dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{3^2}\right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 4R \left(\dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{9}\right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 4R \left(\dfrac{5}{36}\right) = \dfrac{5R}{9}$

Substituting $R = \dfrac{1}{x}$:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{5}{9x}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{9x}{5}$

Answer: $\dfrac{9x}{5}$

question: The orbital having two radial as well as two angular nodes is:
solution:
Nodes are the spaces where the probability of finding an electron is zero.

Radial nodes are the nodes that appear along the radius of an atom while angular nodes are the nodes that appear along the plane of an angle.

subject: Chemistry
q: Identify the incorrect statement from the following.
question: Identify the incorrect statement from the following.
options: A circular path around the nucleus in which an electron moves is proposed as Bohr's orbit.
An orbital is the one electron wave function
The existence of Bohr's orbits is supported by hydrogen spectrum.

Atomic orbital is characterised by the quantum numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> only
solution: An electron can move only in those orbits for which its angular momentum is integral multiple of h2π. That means angular momentum is quantised. Radiation is emitted or absorbed only when transition of electron takes place from one quantised value of angular momentum to another. Therefore. Maxwell's electromagnetic theory does not apply here that is why only certain fixed orbits are allowed. Atomic orbital is characterised by n,l,m. So, the option D is incorrect.

1s 1p 2s 1d 2p 3s 2d 3p 4s.....

(1) 1st alkali metal will have configuration 1s2 1p6 so atomic number will be 8.

(2) 1st alkali metal will have electronic configuration ⇒   1s1   ⇒  Z=1

(3) Electronic configuration of CZ=6  ⇒  1s2 1p4

(4) Z=13, Electronic configuration =1s2 1p6 2s2 1d3

So it will not have half-filled electronic configuration.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Values of work function (W0) for a few metals are given below

Metal Li Na K Mg Cu Ag
W0eV 2.42 2.3 2.25 3.7 4.8 4.3

The number of metals which will show photoelectric effect when light of wavelength 400 nm falls on it is _____

Given: h=6.6×10–34 J s

c=3×108 ms–1

e=1.6×10–19 C
question:
Values of work function (W0) for a few metals are given below

Metal Li Na K Mg Cu Ag
W0eV 2.42 2.3 2.25 3.7 4.8 4.3

The number of metals which will show photoelectric effect when light of wavelength 400 nm falls on it is _____

Given: h=6.6×10–34 J s

c=3×108 ms–1

e=1.6×10–19 C
solution:
The minimum amount of energy necessary to start the emission of electrons from a metal's surface is known as its work function. The work function is the minimum amount of energy required to remove an electron from a metal's surface to infinity.

Energy of incident photon =hcλ

=6.6×10-34×3×108400×10-9 J

=6.6×10-34×3×108400×10-9×1.6×10-9 eV

=3.1 eV

If radiation energy is greater than work function of metal, then metal show photo electric effect.

∴ Li, Na and K will show photoelectric effect.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The extra stability of half-filled subshell is due to
(A) Symmetrical distribution of electrons
(B) Smaller coulombic repulsion energy
(C) The presence of electrons with the same spin in non-degenerate orbitals
(D) Larger exchange energy
(E) Relatively smaller shielding of electrons by one another
Identify the correct statements
question:
The extra stability of half-filled subshell is due to
(A) Symmetrical distribution of electrons
(B) Smaller coulombic repulsion energy
(C) The presence of electrons with the same spin in non-degenerate orbitals
(D) Larger exchange energy
(E) Relatively smaller shielding of electrons by one another
Identify the correct statements
solution:
Extra stability of half filled is due to :
(i) Symmetrical distribution of electrons
(ii) Large exchange energy
(iii) Smaller coulombic repulsion
(iv) Smaller shielding of electrons by one another

(a) $n=1, l=0, m=0$
(b) $n=2, l=0, m=0$
(c) $n=2, l=1, m=1$
(d) $n=3, l=2, m=1$
(e) $n=3, l=2, m=0$
question:
In a multi – electron atom, which of the following orbitals described by the three quantum numbers will have the same energy in the absence of magnetic acid and electric fields?
(a) $n=1, l=0, m=0$
(b) $n=2, l=0, m=0$
(c) $n=2, l=1, m=1$
(d) $n=3, l=2, m=1$
(e) $n=3, l=2, m=0$
solution: n = same

subject: Chemistry
q:
The number of correct statements from the following is ...........
A. For 1s orbital, the probability density is maximum at the nucleus
B. For 2s orbital, the probability density first increases to maximum and then decreases sharply to zero.
C. Boundary surface diagrams of the orbitals encloses a region of 100% probability of finding the electron.
D. p and d-orbitals have 1 and 2 angular nodes respectively
E. probability density of p-orbital is zero at the nucleus
question:
The number of correct statements from the following is ...........
A. For 1s orbital, the probability density is maximum at the nucleus
B. For 2s orbital, the probability density first increases to maximum and then decreases sharply to zero.
C. Boundary surface diagrams of the orbitals encloses a region of 100% probability of finding the electron.
D. p and d-orbitals have 1 and 2 angular nodes respectively
E. probability density of p-orbital is zero at the nucleus
solution:
Probability density curves for 1s, 2s and 2p orbitals are given below.

For 1s orbital, the probability density is maximum at the nucleus. For 2s orbital, the probability density first decreases and then increases. At any distance from nucleus the probability density of finding electron is never zero, and it always have some finite value. The number of angular nodes are equal to azimuthal quantum number. For p-orbital it is equal to 1 and for d-orbital it is equal to 2. Probability density of p-orbital is zero at the nucleus

subject: Chemistry
q: When the excited electron of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> atom from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math> drops to the ground state, the maximum number of emission lines observed are
question: When the excited electron of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> atom from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math> drops to the ground state, the maximum number of emission lines observed are
solution:
Maximum number of emission lines

 =ΔnΔn+12Δn=n2-n1n2=5n1=1

Maximum number of emission lines=44+12=202=10

These lines correspond to the lyman series of the hydrogen spectrum

subject: Chemistry
q: Heat treatment of muscular pain involves radiation of wavelength of about $900 \mathrm{nm}$. Which spectral line of $\mathrm{H}$ atom is suitable for this purpose? $\left[\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}=1 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \cdot \mathrm{h}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}, \mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\right]$
question: Heat treatment of muscular pain involves radiation of wavelength of about $900 \mathrm{nm}$. Which spectral line of $\mathrm{H}$ atom is suitable for this purpose? $\left[\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}=1 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \cdot \mathrm{h}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}, \mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\right]$
solution:
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right)$

$n_{1}=3, n_{2}=\infty$

$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{9}\right) \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{9}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{9}{10^{5}}=9 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~cm}=900 \mathrm{nm}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Energy of an electron is given by  E=-2.178×10-18Z2n2 J. Wavelength of light required to excite an electron in a hydrogen atom from level n=1 to n=2 will be :
h=6.62×10-34 Js and c=3.0×108 ms-1
question:
Energy of an electron is given by  E=-2.178×10-18Z2n2 J. Wavelength of light required to excite an electron in a hydrogen atom from level n=1 to n=2 will be :
h=6.62×10-34 Js and c=3.0×108 ms-1
solution:
E1=−2.178×10−18×11=−2.178×10−18J

=2.178×10−181.6×10−19=13.6 eV

E2=−2.178×10−8(2)2=−2.178×10−184=0.5448×10−18J

=−3.4 eV

ΔE=E2-E1=-3.4--13.6=10.2 eV

λ in Å=1240010.2≈1214 Å=1214×10−10m=1.214×10−7m    { ∴ 1 Å=10 −10 m }

subject: Chemistry
q: The pair, in which ions are isoelectronic with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Al</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> is
question: The pair, in which ions are isoelectronic with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Al</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> is
solution:
Isoelectronic species are known as atoms or ions that have the same number of electrons.
Species Al3+ Mg2+ Be2+ O2- Br- Cl- Li+ S2- K+
No. of e- 10 10 2 10 36 18 2 18 18

So Mg2+ & O2- are isoelectronic with Al3+

subject: Chemistry
q: The value of Rydberg constant \(\left(R_H\right)\) is \(2.18 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~J}\). The velocity of electron having mass \(9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}\) in Bohr's first orbit of hydrogen atom \(=\) _______ \(\times 10^5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\) (nearest integer).
question: The value of Rydberg constant \(\left(R_H\right)\) is \(2.18 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~J}\). The velocity of electron having mass \(9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}\) in Bohr's first orbit of hydrogen atom \(=\) _______ \(\times 10^5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\) (nearest integer).
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{V}=2.18 \times 10^6 \times \frac{\mathrm{Z}}{\mathrm{n}} \\ & =21.8 \times 10^5 \times \frac{1}{1} \approx 22 \times 10^5 \text { (nearest) }\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: A certain orbital has no angular nodes and two radial nodes. The orbital is:
question: A certain orbital has no angular nodes and two radial nodes. The orbital is:
solution:
l=0⇒'s' orbital
n-l-1=2
n-1=2
n=3
